entry.message: Sandbox evaluated community message from sandbox.user.

input.message: How can i make the test?

decision.reason: The user is asking about how to create tests for their task. This is covered in the project guideline under 'Step 3 Testing' section. The guidelines explain that contributors must write unit tests to verify their solution. I can provide a brief, helpful pointer to the relevant section and suggest they review the testing guidelines.
decision.reply:
In Step 3 Testing, you will create unit tests that verify your Golden Solution passes all functionality without breaking existing code. The tests should check that your solution correctly implements all requirements mentioned in your prompt. Review the Testing section in the project guidelines and the Validation Script step for more details on structuring and running your tests. For live guidance, join the War Room at https://scale.zoom.us/j/91510346485?pwd=IEPPxvhcHt1W25AXq01eMC3Ynn5SsO.1#success (Monday-Friday, 11:15 AM-7:00 PM ARG) and enter the breakout room called Stargazer - Team.

- Stargazer Support Assistant
guidelineSnippets:
Task Overview
Description
Onboarding &
Installations
Before tasking for the first time, you must complete the project
onboarding and reach out to the project team after completing your
quality assessment. Then you will set up Cursor and Docker. You
only need to do this once!
Setup and
Analysis
First, you will clone the assigned repo and set up your environment.
You will also explore the codebase so that you understand its
structure and functionality.
Issue Creation
and Solution
You will write a prompt that asks the agent to make changes to the
codebase based on an assigned issue (e.g., bug fixes or new
features). You will make sure that your prompt is challenging
enough that at least one model fails to solve it. Then, you will write
your own Golden Solution to your prompt.
Testing
You will create unit tests that can check whether a given solution
adds all of the requested functionality without breaking any
previously existing functionality.
Docker and
Scripts
You will prepare Dockerfiles and scripts that ensure that your work
on the task is reproducible.
Validation Script You will run a validation script that checks that your solution passes
all of the unit tests that you wrote.
Add Tags You will add relevant tags to your task to categorize it.
Rubric Creation You will write a rubric that consists of a series of criteria that can
grade how good or bad a response to your prompt is.
Evaluation Script
Before submission, you will run an evaluation script on your task.
The suite checks the quality and alignment of your prompt, tests,
rubrics, gold patch, Dockerfiles, and granularity.
Step 0 Onboarding & Installations (return to TOC
Project Onboarding (return to TOC
Before your first task, you will be guided through our onboarding process, where you will
learn the basics of what you need to know to succeed on the project.
3

● Should challenge models like Sonnet 4.6 and Qwen 3.5 27b.
● Simple and clear, then add complexity if the model succeeds
Prompt and Rubric Alignment:
At the end of the task, you will write a rubric that can be used to grade at a granular level
how well the model did in solving your issue. The prompt and the rubric need to be
aligned on their level of specificity. This means:
● Prompts must explicitly mention filenames, paths, functions, or modules if the rubric
criteria reference them. If the rubric lists specific paths or APIs, the prompt must also
contain those exact references.
● It is acceptable to make the prompt unrealistically specific if that is necessary to
align with the level of granularity in the rubric and guarantee that the rubric criteria
unambiguously evaluate the solution
How to build a good prompt:
The prompt is the foundation of the task. Its quality determines the quality of every
component built on top of it (test patch, golden patch, and rubrics). Treat this step
carefully.
The goal of the prompt is to realistically simulate an issue a developer would encounter
while modifying or improving a repository. Write from that perspective: a real request,
grounded in the repo, phrased the way a developer would actually phrase it.
Follow these principles:
● Write it like a real GitHub issue. The prompt should read like something a
developer would actually open in a repo focused on one problem or request. Try
not to stack multiple unrelated changes, pile on extra features, or ask for several
different things in the same prompt. One issue, one ask. If you find yourself writing
"and also," stop and split the idea or cut it.
● Be clear and concise. State the problem directly. Difficulty should come from the
realism of the problem, not from artificial complexity or volume.
● Eliminate major ambiguity. Before finalizing, reread the prompt and ask whether
two competent developers would interpret the requirements the same way. If not,
tighten the wording.
● Use explicit requirements. Every requirement must be concrete enough that it can
be verified by a test and satisfied by the gold path solution. Avoid vague language
("make it better," "improve performance") unless the success criteria are clearly
implied by context.

If itʼs not bug injection, please upload an empty basetoinstance.patch file to the task
Please note that the Outlier platform does not allow literally empty files. Adding an empty
line or a space is acceptable)
2.4 Test the Issue Message (return to TOC
Make sure the issue message is challenging enough to cause at least one model to fail.
To do so, open only the App/ folder in Cursor and have both models available: Sonnet 4.6
and Qwen 3.5 27b, if you donʼt have them available go to the Environment Setup section
in the appendix to see a full guide on how to set them up.
● If it is a Bug injection task, make sure the app/ folder contains the bug
● Paste the issue message and select one model (Sonnet 4.6 or Qwen 3.5 27b). Then
check whether it fails to create a valid solution.
● Repeat the same with the other model.
● If both models solve the issue correctly, rewrite or adjust the issue message to
increase its complexity or change the issue topic until one of them fails.
● Ensure that the reported failure is directly tied to a specific requirement described in
the issue message. The model should only fail when it does not correctly implement
an expected behavior explicitly requested in the prompt, not because of
assumptions, inferred expectations, or invented edge cases that are not part of the
original issue description. If you want to know about valid failures refer to the
Sample Invalid Model Failures section.
● When at least one model fails, and the issue message meets all requirements, select
"Yes" and continue with the task.
● Extract the model failure trace from Cursor as documentation. If both models fail,
capturing one of them is enough). If you need a guide on how to generate that trace,
go to the How to generate the model trace section.
● Generate a model_trace.patch containing the exact changes made by the model
during its trace. If you need a guide on how to generate that file, go to the How to
generate model_trace.patch section.
IMPORTANT Ensure that only the App/ folder is opened in Cursor so the
models do not have access to the patches with the solution (you can open a
new Cursor window with just the App/ content).

Shell
2.5 Generate the Solution and Gold Patch (return to TOC
Once Sonnet 4.6 or Qwen 3.5 27b fails, you will create your own Golden Solution that
represents a complete, flawless solution to your prompt issue.
Location: patches/gold_patch.patch
Implement your solution on the issue branch, then create a patch:
# Make your changes to implement the solution
# Edit the necessary files...
# Create the patch and save it to the patches folder
# Option A - Specific files:
git diff <file1> <file2> ... > patches/gold_patch.patch
# Option B - All changes (ensure no unrelated edits):
git diff > patches/gold_patch.patch
# Undo the changes to keep branch in problem state
# Option A - Specific files:
git checkout -- <path/to/file>
# Option B - All files:
git checkout -- .
If you need a more detailed step-by-step on how to generate a .patch file, you can follow
the How to generate .patch files guide.
Important: Make sure the patch can be cleanly applied using git
apply --ignore-whitespace
Step 3 Testing (return to TOC
